data.preview:
i wish to add a side-project to this repo.
i want to make a "rocket FPV drone" in the simulator that it's job is to hit a helium balloon that is floating up and sideways with the wind. a rocket-fpv-drone is a regular fpv drone that its fixed camera is pointing straight up (+z direction in the drone's/body reference frame).
since this repo already has the physics simulation of the fpv drone- i want to make a classic controller for hitting the balloon.
the inputs for the controller should be: 
1. position of the target/balloon in the frame - where the frame center is the origin and the edges are +-1. 
2. direction unit vector from the rocket drone to the target/balloon + convertion of this dir-vec to elevation and heading (spherical coordinates). this should be in world reference frame.

i want to test my pid controller in the simulator and refine it.

please go over the repo, check what is already implemented, check if there is something missing for this project, understand what this repo is capable and write a plan file called "rocket_drone_vs_hellium_balloon_plan.md" under the root of this repo. this plan should say what is the goal of this project, how does it plan to achieve this goal, what can it use from this repo and what does in need to add.

my pid controller controls the sticks of the rocket drone as its actuators, so no need for you to implement anything. i will add it later.

the there should be several render modes:
1. FPV mode - we "see" what the drone sees. a circle in the size of the balloon in the balloon position in the frame. and a text in the top left side of the frame with heading and elevation of the dir-vec.
2. Side View / World view mode - we see the drone and the balloon in any angle we want which is between the x-z plane to the y-z plane. we can rotate arround the z axis. we see the drone as a point with a velocity vector and the balloon with a veclocity vector and their trail which is slowly disappearing (the older the trail segment, the more transparent it is).

anything else is missing? do you have any information missing to write the plan?
data.name: Add plan for rocket drone vs balloon

data.preview:
i want to be able to use the vscode-copilot-chat from an external extension/app/program/script.
the idea is that the chat itself (without the code changes will be accessible. meaning that an outer program can read the model's response and can send prompts to the model like the user is sending.
a kind of bridge between vscode-copilot-chat to the outside world.
some application for this kind of bridge: a script with telegram bot which lets the user write prompts from telegram and send them to the copilot chat and recieve what the chat writes back - in telegram. giving the user the ability to work with copilot chat from anywhere!

my questions:
1. is this even possible? meaning, without changing the code of this repo, just installing a vscode-copilot-bridge extension that will do that. is this kind of bridge possible? can we program it given this repo?
2. if it is possible, can you please go over this repo and write a comprehensive guide of how it should be done? with example, sources of code from this repo and a path to them so the agent that builds this extension will know where to look. 
this guide will be in:
C:\Users\tamipinhasi\Documents\repos\vscode-copilot-bridge-comprehensive -guide.md
3. after you finish the guide, go over it and in its end, add a plan. milestones and tasks with a checkbox next to each task and placeholder for time of finish.
data.name: Document Copilot bridge guide

data.preview:
bird's eye view project.
lets plan this project.
the end goals are:
1. estimate translation of the drone (ego/self) with a camera pointing to the minus Z axis of the drone (body reference frame).
2. align perfectly the frame, even though there is a movement (rotation and translation) between frames.
3. detect motion using the aligned frames difference.
4. make a mini-map by stitching all the bird's eye view frames.

setup:
an FPV drone with betaflight flashed on the flight controller (FC).
a raspberry pi is the compute module. a camera sits on the bottom plate of the fpv drone, pointing down. the long horizontal FoV of the resolution of the camera is from back to front. the short vertical Fov of the resolution is from left to right.
the camera is always pointing in the minus Z axis of the drone (from up to down in the drone/body reference frame).
we are given the camera calibration (intrinsic matrix and distortion coefficients of the camera).

utilities:
the pi is connected via UART to the FC. 
can read the attitude (euler angles) from the betaflightMSP library that already exists (betaflightMSP.h betaflightMSP.cpp).

assumptions:
the ground is far away. meaning, the translation is tiny between frames.
the pi is not that computationally capable so we should be as efficient as possible with the way we run the algorithms. for instance: to align 2 consecutive frames, we will first use opencv's warpPerspective. we will convert the attitude to a rotation matrix. then, we will use the rotation matrix to make both frames to be shown like they were taken with the camera pointing to the minus Z axis in the world reference frame (bird's eye view- BEV).
in the first step, we will want to make a webapp which shows this warped frame.
in the second step, we will want to find keypoints in one frame and find them in the other frame so we can align the image perfectly on top of each other (how do you call this alignment in computer vision?). to match the keypoint from one frame to the other, we will have to be efficient.
we will assume the translation is small: we will do template matching (the size of the template will be in the config file) and look for the template not in the entire other frame, but only in a crop of the other frame. we will assume that the template did not move too much so the template should be around where it was in the previous frame (where it was taken from). this way, the template matching will be much faster because we look for the template in a crop. the crop size should also be a parameter in the config file.
after finding the template, we should take into account that the position of the template we found was in a crop. so we need to give the template matching its real global pixel coordinate in the BEV warpped frame. this is a way to make this more efficient.
we can make it even more efficient- we can take a stride of 2 of the template and of the frame we are looking the template in, and get a course template matching. after getting the course template matching, we can get a fine match with no stride to a tighter crop.
we can have the parameters in the config:
number of keypoints, course crop size, fine crop size, template size.
course crop size will be the effective size. its real size will be 2 times smaller because of the stride.
this is very effective!
now that we have been tracking several keypoints, we can estimate the movement/velocity of the drone in pixels (pixel offset and direction and yaw/heading rotation). we can get a better estimation of where the crop should be in the next frame. so we can center the crop around the position + delta_time* estimated pixel_velocity. we should have a cap to the value of delta_time* estimated pixel_velocity in the config file: max_distance_from_keypoint_center. if delta_time* estimated pixel_velocity is larger than max_distance_from_keypoint_center, then its value will be max_distance_from_keypoint_center.

now we have translation estimation.
but we also have several keypoints, and the camera calibration. now we will try to align the images and stich them in a way that they will sit right on top of each other perfectly. all the keypoints will align. how do we do that? given that we have tracked the keypoints from before, what opencv function should we use?

now that we have the aligned frames, we want to take the difference between the frames and mark the changes. the frames difference. once we have it, we will make it look like an event camera: all the -255 to 0 will be shown in blue as a half transparent mask on the frame in the webapp. and from 0 to 255 will be shown in red (like the blue, only in red).
the config file will have parameters of: determining alpha - the transparency factor, determining a threshold. a value that any movement under this value, will not be shown. if the threshold of movement is 30. and the movement is -29 or 29, it will be zeroed. anything between -30 to 30 will be zeroed in the movement mask. the rest will keep being continious between -255 to -31 and between 30 to 255.
now we have the movement mask on top of the real time frame.

finally, we need to use the frames and the keypoints tracking to stich the frames together to a mini-map.
we want to have a map that as we keep on flying, more data is added up to it and we can have a kind of map like in strategy games. do you understand what i mean? so we stitch the BEV frames in a way that they will create the BEV map.
this map will keep on updating.

lets write a plan for this project.
answer the questions i had with a Q&A in the document of the plan.md file and also write your own questions of things that needs clarification.
if you have any suggestions, add them as suggestions, so it will be clear that this is what you are suggesting.
lets plan!
data.name: Plan bird's-eye view pipeline

lets make a plan for this project. i added relevant files in the folder "additional files". one is a manual and the others are relevant files from betaflight's  firmware.
this project's goal is to stream the video from a camera connected to a CM4 with the lowest latency possible and lay on top of it a canvas with the OSD of betaflight. the cm4 is connected via UART to the flight controller which is flashed with betaflight.
the BetaflightMSP files are files that i had from a different project - they are meant to communicate with the flight controller. i guess there are several commands missing there. we will need to add them. but first we will need to understand what is missing.

so now that you know the end goal, read:
additional_files\betaflight_OSD_display_port_detailed_guide.md
and write a plan for this project. with tasks and sub-tasks with checkboxes so we can track our progress.
we can use python and picamera2 to get the least latency preview of the camera feed.

if you need to look anything online, please do. 
lets start planning.

if i want to get an msp packet with all the osd values and configuration so i can put an hd osd canvas on my video feed - where does it happen in befaflight's code? what file takes care of it? how does the packet frame looks like?
can you write me here in the chat everything without changing the code?

i want to make a plan with you that characterize this repo, especially:
src\core\processes_coordinator.py
and the optimization tricks done in:
src\utils\imu_tracker.py

the idea is that if we had to redo this project from scratch, what would we take out of it? how would we structure it? what will be a good document that will serve us to rewrite this repo better with new features?

lets try to make such an md file under the root folder.
lets discuss it in high level first and optimize this document together.
priority is low latency. trade-off is accuracy.
we want fast response to the frames and to the tracking but if the tracking is too slow, the response will be slow, even though the tracking will be more accurate.
also, we use shared memory (shm) to reduce the overhead of serializing and de-serializing messages and frames.
we have several processes so we can run everything needed in parallel.
do you have more to add?
can you figure out the optimization trick in the imu tracker?

apparently there is a problem with the Z_EKF implementation in the src\main\flight\position.c file.
here are the instructions to fix it:
Would I change the Z_EKF implementation? Yes — a few specific things
A) Add “recovery” behavior when gating is stuck

Right now, if you start rejecting baro continuously, the EKF can run open-loop and drift → divergence.

Fix: detect a reject streak and temporarily relax.
Example strategy (simple + effective):

Keep a counter rejCount

If gateRejected for > N frames (e.g. 20–50 frames):

temporarily inflate R (e.g. R *= 50), and allow update

or temporarily increase gateSigma for a short window

or do a “soft reset”: set z = zMeas, v unchanged, keep biases

This is the single biggest robustness improvement for “windows / pressure steps”.

B) Make S robust: covariance floor / avoid overconfidence collapse

Your failure mode screams: S becomes too small, so the gate becomes tiny, so you reject forever.

Add a floor like:

S = max(S, S_min) where S_min maybe corresponds to (0.3m)^2 etc.
or

enforce minimum Pzz / Pvv floors

Also ensure you maintain P symmetry after updates:

P = 0.5*(P + P^T) occasionally
and ideally use Joseph-form covariance update in ekfUpdateZ() / ekfUpdateV() to keep P PSD under numerical error.

C) Handle “baro step” explicitly via the baro-bias state bb

You already have innov = zMeas - (z + bb) which is great.
But if bb can’t move fast enough, gating turns it off.

Two options:

Increase Qbb (parameter) — you’re doing that.

Add logic: if innovation looks like a step (large, persistent, same sign) → treat it as bb event.

Implementation sketch:

if |innov| > stepThresh AND |d/dt zMeas| large, then:

update bb more aggressively (temporary higher Qbb or direct bb += alpha*innov)

D) Don’t do velocity update when Z was rejected for a while

You currently do ekfUpdateV() only when Z accepted (good).
But you also gate vario update on r33>0.8 (good).
One improvement:

If Z has been rejected for K frames, skip V update even if r33 good, because vBaro is derived from the same disturbed baro.

E) dt correctness and scheduling

You assume fixed:

const float dt = HZ_TO_INTERVAL(TASK_ALTITUDE_RATE_HZ);


If actual task jitter exists, Q scaling is wrong and can destabilize confidence.
If Betaflight gives you actual delta time for the task, use it. If not, at least sanity clamp:

dt = constrain(dt, 0.5*nominal, 2.0*nominal)

F) Debug values: log the thing that matters

Right now you log innovZ, gateRejected, etc.
Add one more that will immediately tell you why gating happens:

log sqrt(S) (or limit) and/or S scaled

log R_eff
Without that, you’re partially blind.

3) Devil’s advocate: your gating logic can create a “deadlock”

Your gate uses:

limit = gateSigma * sqrt(S)
reject if |innov| > limit


But S depends on P and R.
If your filter ever becomes too confident (P small, R small), the gate gets tighter → more rejects → fewer updates → P can stay “wrong” → deadlock.

That’s why recovery logic (A) and P floors (B) are not optional in real flight.

If you want the highest ROI single code change

Add reject-streak recovery + log limit and R_eff.

That alone will prevent “stuck reject → divergence” and will make tuning dramatically easier.

If you paste your ekfPredict / ekfUpdateZ / ekfUpdateV / state definition (the P matrix layout), I can be concrete about:

which P elements to floor

recommended Joseph update

best place to inject bb-step handling

notice that there are only 8 debug elements in the debug array. if we need more that what we have, we can skip the R[3,3] and the accel_z. they are not really needed for the debug.

please follow the instructions and fix the Z_EKF. are the instructions clear? do you have any questions before you start?
data.name: Improve Z_EKF recovery and gating
